allergens: gluten (soy sauce, cornstarch thickener may contain wheat)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of hot and sour soup is approximately 300 g (one bowl), as typically served in Chinese restaurants in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Hot and Sour Soup — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy / Foundation Foods composite estimate
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Chinese restaurant-style hot and sour soup exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN. Values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for 'Soup, hot and sour, Chinese restaurant' (FDC ID 173568) and cross-referenced with USDA Foundation Foods ingredient-level data for tofu, egg, mushroom, bamboo shoot, vinegar, and pork. Per-100g values are derived from this composite. The restaurant version served at 'See Ya Tonight' includes Sichuan-style black pepper and vinegar; sodium is adjusted upward to reflect typical restaurant preparation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; analogous estimate based on mixed vegetable and tofu soup category
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Chinese hot and sour soup in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate based on: (1) mixed vegetable soups with tofu and starch thickener typically fall in the GI 30–45 range per University of Sydney GI Database entries for comparable mixed soups; (2) the primary glycemic contributors are cornstarch thickener and bamboo shoots, both low-GI ingredients; (3) vinegar content further suppresses postprandial glucose response per peer-reviewed evidence (Johnston et al., 2004, JADA). GL per serving = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (35 × 11.4) / 100 ≈ 4.0.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~420 mg per 100 g; ~1260 mg per 300 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit hot and sour soup to small portions or request reduced-sodium preparation, as a single restaurant bowl can contribute over 50% of the recommended daily sodium intake of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~35) and moderate carbohydrate content (~3.8 g per 100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Hot and sour soup has a low glycemic index and a low glycemic load per serving (~4.0), making it a generally suitable choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; however, the cornstarch thickener and portion size should be monitored to avoid cumulative carbohydrate load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate saturated fat from egg and pork components
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The high sodium content (~1260 mg per serving) poses a cardiovascular risk for individuals with existing heart disease; choosing a lower-sodium version and limiting frequency of consumption is advisable as part of a heart-healthy dietary pattern.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Hot and sour soup (酸辣汤, Suān là tāng) is a classic Chinese soup dish with origins in Sichuan and Hunan cuisine, widely popular across China and in Chinese restaurants throughout Southeast Asia including Singapore. The soup is characterised by its dual flavour profile: tartness from rice vinegar or black vinegar, and heat from white pepper or chilli. The broth is typically made from chicken or pork stock and thickened with cornstarch to achieve a silky, slightly viscous texture. Standard ingredients include silken or firm tofu, wood ear mushrooms (black fungus), bamboo shoots, egg ribbons (created by drizzling beaten egg into the hot broth), and thin strips of pork or chicken. Soy sauce and sesame oil are added for depth and fragrance. The version served at 'See Ya Tonight' (奶奶家酸辣汤) is described as a traditional homemade-style preparation with black pepper included, emphasising digestive and appetite-stimulating properties. Nutritionally, hot and sour soup is relatively low in calories (~38 kcal per 100 g) and has a low glycemic index (~35), making it suitable for weight-conscious and diabetic diners. However, sodium content is high (~420 mg per 100 g), which is a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The soup provides moderate protein from tofu and egg, small amounts of iron and calcium, and beneficial compounds from mushrooms and vinegar. It is commonly consumed as a starter or light meal in Chinese dining contexts.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for this specific restaurant dish exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for Chinese restaurant hot and sour soup (FDC ID 173568), which is the closest approved-source match. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database category for comparable mixed soups. Confidence is medium rather than low because the USDA SR Legacy entry is a direct category match for Chinese restaurant hot and sour soup, and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy 'Soup, hot and sour, Chinese restaurant' (FDC ID 173568). This is the closest approved-source match available. 2. Sodium value (~420 mg/100g) reflects typical restaurant preparation; home-prepared versions may be lower. 3. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source entry for this soup. 4. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source entry for this soup. 5. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source entry for this soup. 6. trans_fat_g is 0.0; confirmed as negligible/not detected in the source data for this soup category. 7. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for hot and sour soup exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. Vinegar content is documented to suppress postprandial glycaemia (Johnston et al., 2004). 8. Halal status is listed as false because the dish may contain pork; restaurant-specific halal certification was not confirmed from the field survey source data. 9. The input food_name '奶奶家酸辣汤 Hot and Sour Soup' has been normalised to canonical English 'Hot and Sour Soup'; the Chinese restaurant-specific branding '奶奶家' (Grandma's) is preserved in aliases.
